patches.hypothesis: The original patch overfits by introducing specific checks for interfaces and abstract methods, which may not be suitable for all scenarios. A more generalized approach is needed to determine whether to call the real method, potentially leveraging existing mocking framework features.
patches.original_hypothesis: The error occurs because the method attempts to call a real method on an abstract method, which is not allowed. We need to check if the method is abstract before attempting to call it.

patches.hypothesis: The issue arises because the current implementation tries to call real methods on abstract methods, which is not allowed. The fix involves checking if the method is abstract before attempting to call it. Since the 'isAbstract' method is not available, we can use the Modifier class from java.lang.reflect to check if the method is abstract.
patches.changes:
- return invocation.callRealMethod();
+ if ((invocation.getMethod().getModifiers() & java.lang.reflect.Modifier.ABSTRACT) != 0) { return null; } else { return invocation.callRealMethod(); }
patches.diff:
- return invocation.callRealMethod();
+ if ((invocation.getMethod().getModifiers() & java.lang.reflect.Modifier.ABSTRACT) != 0) { return null; } else { return invocation.callRealMethod(); }
patches.original_code:
/** * Optional Answer that adds partial mocking support *

* {@link Answer} can be used to define the return values of unstubbed invocations. *

* This implementation can be helpful when working with legacy code. * When this implementation is used, unstubbed methods will delegate to the real implementation. * This is a way to create a partial mock object that calls real methods by default. *

* As usual you are going to read the partial mock warning: * Object oriented programming is more less tackling complexity by dividing the complexity into separate, specific, SRPy objects. * How does partial mock fit into this paradigm? Well, it just doesn't... * Partial mock usually means that the complexity has been moved to a different method on the same object. * In most cases, this is not the way you want to design your application. *

* However, there are rare cases when partial mocks come handy: * dealing with code you cannot change easily (3rd party interfaces, interim refactoring of legacy code etc.) * However, I wouldn't use partial mocks for new, test-driven & well-designed code. *

*/ public Object answer(InvocationOnMock invocation) throws Throwable { return invocation.callRealMethod(); }

patches.hypothesis: The error occurs because the method tries to call a real method that is abstract, which is not allowed. We need to check if the method is abstract and return a default value instead of calling the real method.
patches.changes:
- return invocation.callRealMethod();
+ if (invocation.getMethod().getDeclaringClass().isInterface() || Modifier.isAbstract(invocation.getMethod().getModifiers())) { return null; } else { return invocation.callRealMethod(); }
patches.diff:
- return invocation.callRealMethod();
+ if (invocation.getMethod().getDeclaringClass().isInterface() || Modifier.isAbstract(invocation.getMethod().getModifiers())) { return null; } else { return invocation.callRealMethod(); }
patches.original_code:
/** * Optional Answer that adds partial mocking support *

* {@link Answer} can be used to define the return values of unstubbed invocations. *

* This implementation can be helpful when working with legacy code. * When this implementation is used, unstubbed methods will delegate to the real implementation. * This is a way to create a partial mock object that calls real methods by default. *

* As usual you are going to read the partial mock warning: * Object oriented programming is more less tackling complexity by dividing the complexity into separate, specific, SRPy objects. * How does partial mock fit into this paradigm? Well, it just doesn't... * Partial mock usually means that the complexity has been moved to a different method on the same object. * In most cases, this is not the way you want to design your application. *

* However, there are rare cases when partial mocks come handy: * dealing with code you cannot change easily (3rd party interfaces, interim refactoring of legacy code etc.) * However, I wouldn't use partial mocks for new, test-driven & well-designed code. *

*/ public Object answer(InvocationOnMock invocation) throws Throwable { return invocation.callRealMethod(); }
